# Source: https://docs.anyscale.com/storage/s3.md

# Access S3 buckets

[View Markdown](/storage/s3.md)

# Access S3 buckets

This section configures S3 access for Anyscale clusters. At a high level, accessing an S3 bucket requires two things:

1. The IAM role needs the [correct policies to allow S3 operations](#iam-policies).
2. The bucket must grant access to the given IAM role.

The first part *is always required*, while the second part may be optional if the IAM role and the S3 bucket are in the *same AWS account*.

info

If you try to read objects created from a cluster running in a different AWS account you may see `Permission Denied`.

To fix this, you can

* Use the `--bucket-owner-full-control` flag from the CLI for `{"ACL": "bucket-owner-full-control"}` to ensure the bucket owner can access the file.
* Enforce bucket owner to assume control of all uploaded content using the [**bucket owner enforced**](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/about-object-ownership.html) configuration in S3.

## Access existing S3 buckets[​](#access-existing-s3-buckets "Direct link to Access existing S3 buckets")

### Required information[​](#required-info "Direct link to Required information")

* <!-- -->
  S3 bucket to use. This `ARN` looks something like `arn:aws:s3:::<BUCKET_NAME>`.
* <!-- -->
  The IAM role that the cluster is running with.

### Determine the cluster's IAM role[​](#determine-the-clusters-iam-role "Direct link to Determine the cluster's IAM role")

By default, Anyscale clusters run with a cloud-specific IAM role. You can also deploy your cluster with a custom IAM role. See [Manage AWS IAM roles for Anyscale clusters](/iam/aws.md).

### Ensure IAM role policies[​](#iam-policies "Direct link to Ensure IAM role policies")

1. Search for your role on the [AWS IAM page](https://console.aws.amazon.com/iamv2/home#/roles) and select it. ![Search For Role](/assets/images/search_for_role-d98331c56afacde3c41907394b5bf500.png)

2. Look in the **Permissions policies** section of your role. If it has `AmazonS3FullAccess`, or a similar custom policy that grants `s3:ListBucket`, `s3:GetObject` & `s3:PutObject` on any resource, you're done. If not, continue to set up the correct policies.

#### Correctly configured:[​](#correctly-configured "Direct link to Correctly configured:")

![Search For role](/assets/images/good_available_permissions-126ff21d9325504e945c6f3476826cc3.png)

#### Missing permissions:[​](#missing-permissions "Direct link to Missing permissions:")

![Search For role](/assets/images/available_permissions-2ab2bb0f6644ee6b8ac901c5f26f8981.png)

3. Click **Attach policies**.

![attach\_policies](/assets/images/attach_policies-a5b601a096e1e88f3c3fcf70cd33da19.png)

4. Search for `AmazonS3FullAccess` and select the policy.

![S3 Full Access](/assets/images/selected_s3_full_access-99a9cd1b72cb9199b068c78f83eb0b4f.png)

5. Click **Attach policy**.

![attach\_policy](/assets/images/attach_policy-bf19c2f59e8b3c8c4503ac1ba25e79bd.png)

### Configure bucket access[​](#bucket-access "Direct link to Configure bucket access")

To access an S3 bucket that exists in a different account, you need to configure that bucket to allow this IAM role access. You can do this by adding the following policy to that S3 bucket:

1. Search for your S3 bucket on the [S3 page](https://s3.console.aws.amazon.com/s3/home) and select it.

![Search For bucket](/assets/images/search_bucket-c81b75b94fa268ffc73f9f9e89650aa3.png)

2. Select the **Permissions** tab.

![Permissions tab](/assets/images/bucket_overview-5108c930fb6b9d4773b8994f8632cb88.png)

3. Look in the **Bucket Policy** section and click **Edit**.

![Bucket Policy Edit](/assets/images/bucket_policy-931e0f474032a9f5d1f426679192eb92.png)

4. Paste the following JSON into the **Policy** editor box. Make sure to replace `<ROLE_ARN>` and `<BUCKET_NAME>` blocks with your role's ARN and your bucket name.

```
{
    "Version": "2012-10-17",
    "Id": "",
    "Statement": [
        {
            "Sid": "ReadWriteS3",
            "Effect": "Allow",
            "Principal": {
                "AWS": "<ROLE_ARN>"
            },
            "Action": [
                "s3:GetObject",
                "s3:ListBucket",
                "s3:PutObject"
            ],
            "Resource": [
                "arn:aws:s3:::<BUCKET_NAME>",
                "arn:aws:s3:::<BUCKET_NAME>/*"
            ]
        }
    ]
}
```

![Policy Editor Box](/assets/images/edit_bucket_json-f6526c68c3e43491b643607b94ece000.png)

5. Click **Save changes**.

![Save changes](data:image/png;base64,)

## Use direct credentials for development[​](#direct-credentials "Direct link to Use direct credentials for development")

warning

Storing credentials directly isn't secure. Use IAM roles for production workloads. Use direct credentials only for development and testing.

### Use AWS credential file[​](#use-aws-credential-file "Direct link to Use AWS credential file")

Store credentials in the AWS credential file:

```
# ~/.aws/credentials
[myprofile]
aws_access_key_id = your_access_key
aws_secret_access_key = your_secret_key
```

Access the bucket using the profile:

```
aws s3 cp your_file.txt s3://bucket/path/ --profile myprofile
```

### Pass credentials in code[​](#pass-credentials-in-code "Direct link to Pass credentials in code")

```
import boto3

# Explicitly provide credentials
s3_client = boto3.client(
    's3',
    aws_access_key_id='YOUR_ACCESS_KEY',
    aws_secret_access_key='YOUR_SECRET_KEY',
    aws_session_token='YOUR_SESSION_TOKEN'  # optional
)

# Download file
bucket_name = 'my-dataset-bucket'
object_key = 'datasets/my_data.csv'
destination_file = 'my_data.csv'

s3_client.download_file(bucket_name, object_key, destination_file)
print(f"Downloaded {object_key} to {destination_file}")
```

caution

Setting `AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID` and `AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY` as cluster-wide environment variables blocks access to `$ANYSCALE_ARTIFACT_STORAGE` as they override default credentials. To use both, pass credentials directly to API calls instead of setting cluster-wide environment variables.
